meta.note: Companion material folded out of the curated flow's cuts section. Account-neutral: the discovery brief is a template to fill per account, objections and follow-ups are patterns observed in the source call.

discoveryBrief.title: Discovery brief — fill in before the demo
discoveryBrief.sourceNote: In the source recording, the first ~23 minutes (moments 1–21) were discovery, intros, and context — deliberately kept out of the screen flow. Capture the same ground per account here.
prompts: Who is in the room, and who owns the decision?
How are international workers paid today (wires? per-payment fees? which banks)?
Which countries are in play, and which engagement model per country (contractor / EOR / future subsidiary)?
Roughly how many workers per country, and are any converting from contractor to employee?
Does anyone live in a different country than their nationality suggests? (Residency drives the seat's country and cost.)
What are the current pain points: fees, compliance worry, IP protection, onboarding speed?
Any known statutory concerns in their countries (mandatory allowances, pensions, healthcare)?
What would make this a win for the person running the evaluation?

objections.title: Objection notes
objections.sourceNote: Moment 45 in the source recording (~00:49:53–00:50:59) was a pricing/cost Q&A held out of the core flow. These are the recurring objections and the framings that worked.

items.objection: What does this really cost per country?
items.response: Don't improvise numbers. Use the compensation calculator (flow step 18) live: salary in, employer all-in cost and employee net out. If the prospect's country isn't loaded, name it as a data-load gap and commit to the fix.

items.objection: Isn't healthcare free in these countries?
items.response: Defuser from the source call: “none of this is free — they're paying a lot of taxes.” Statutory healthcare is funded through social charges; supplemental tiers exist on top and can be absorbed or cost-shared.

items.objection: We already pay people by wire — why change?
items.response: Let them do the math on their own wire fees, then add the compliance angle: cross-border wires also signal to those countries that you're paying people there, which can start permanent-establishment questions. One US invoice ends both.

items.objection: What about our IP if you employ them?
items.response: Two-contract chain: IP protection sits in the employment contract (worker ↔ platform as EOR) and in the company-to-company assignment agreement. Offer both documents for attorney review rather than paraphrasing clauses live.

items.objection: Can't we just keep everyone as contractors?
items.response: Surface the classification analysis (flow step 10): the platform shows real risks — fines, penalties — when someone is acting like an employee. The client decides the risk; the platform advises with full information.

followUps.title: Follow-up checklist
followUps.sourceNote: Moments 52–55 in the source recording (~00:57:58–end) were close and follow-up. These are the action-item patterns to confirm after any run of this demo.
items: Confirm country-specific mandatory allowances/stipends for every country in play.
Confirm residency vs nationality for any worker who may live outside their home country — residency drives the seat's country and cost.
Verify the prospect's countries are loaded in the compensation calculator before the next touchpoint.
Resolve any IP-agreement request (client adding their own IP terms) with the implementation team.
Send pricing recap tied to the engagement models discussed (contractor / Contractor Plus / EOR).
Schedule the follow-up with the decision-maker in the room, not just the evaluator.
